question: how to compost pile
title: How to Build a Compost Pile From Scratch
metaDescription: Building a hot compost pile is simpler than people think. Scotty walks you through the exact steps — materials, layering, moisture, and heat — to turn food scraps into rich living soil.

content.opening: People make composting way more complicated than it needs to be. I've seen folks buy expensive bins with airlocks and temperature sensors and proprietary compost starters, and their pile still sits there looking sad and doing nothing. Meanwhile, someone else throws kitchen scraps and yard waste into a pile in the corner of their yard and walks away with the most beautiful finished compost you've ever seen. The difference isn't equipment. It's understanding a few basic principles. Let me walk you through how to build a pile that actually works.
content.directAnswer: A hot compost pile needs four things: a mix of carbon-rich and nitrogen-rich materials in roughly a 25-to-1 ratio, enough moisture to be damp like a wrung-out sponge, enough size to hold heat (at least 3 cubic feet), and regular turning to maintain oxygen flow. Get those four things right and your pile will cook to 130–150 degrees, kill weed seeds and pathogens, and produce finished compost in as little as 4 to 8 weeks.

sections.heading: Day One: What You Need to Get Started
sections.body:
Before you put anything on the ground, gather your materials. You need two categories: carbon and nitrogen.

Carbon materials — the browns — include dry leaves, straw, hay, wood chips, shredded cardboard, and paper bags. These materials are dense with carbon, slow-burning fuel for the microbial community that's about to do the work. They also create physical structure in the pile that allows oxygen to flow through.

Nitrogen materials — the greens — include vegetable scraps, fruit peels, coffee grounds, fresh grass clippings, food waste from the kitchen, and any fresh plant material. These materials are dense with protein and nitrogen, the engine fuel that fires up the microbial community and generates heat.

Here's the most important ratio in composting: 25 to 30 parts carbon for every 1 part nitrogen by weight. The USDA NRCS cites this as the optimal C:N ratio for hot composting. In practical terms, you don't need a scale. The rule of thumb I use is: for every bucket of kitchen scraps or nitrogen-rich material, add two to three generous buckets of dry carbon material. The ratio should lean toward carbon if anything — you can always add more nitrogen material, but a pile that starts too nitrogen-heavy will smell like ammonia and lose fertility.

Get your materials together. Have the carbon material standing by before you start. One of the most common beginner mistakes is adding a lot of food waste without having enough brown material ready to cover it.

sections.heading: Building the Pile: Layering Like a Lasagna
sections.body:
Here's how I start a new pile from nothing.

First, lay down a base layer of carbon material directly on the ground. Wood chips are my favorite — they create excellent drainage and allow air to enter from below. Six inches of carbon material as a base. This matters because you want the bottom of the pile to be airy, not waterlogged.

Next, add your nitrogen layer. Kitchen scraps, vegetable waste, food scraps — whatever nitrogen material you have. Three to four inches. Spread it out across the base so it's not just a lump in the center.

Cover the nitrogen layer with carbon material. I mean cover it completely. No food scraps visible. This is critical for odor control and for keeping the heat balanced across the pile. Another four to six inches of carbon on top.

Repeat this process — nitrogen layer, carbon cover — until your pile is at least three feet high. UMN Extension and Penn State Extension both specify that pile size is essential for heat generation. Below 3 cubic feet, the pile can't insulate itself enough to achieve the thermophilic temperatures that make hot composting effective. You need mass.

I generally build my piles from materials I've accumulated over several days. If you get a big batch of kitchen waste all at once — say, from a restaurant or produce market — build the whole pile that day rather than drizzling material on slowly. A pile that builds quickly tends to heat up faster because all the fresh nitrogen material activates the microbial community simultaneously.

sections.heading: Moisture, Heat, and How to Know It's Working
sections.body:
Two days after building your pile, push your arm into the center.

If the pile is working correctly, the center will be noticeably warm — sometimes uncomfortably hot. You might even see steam rising if you dig into it on a cool morning. That's thermophilic bacteria. That's success.

Temperature in an active hot pile typically reaches 130 to 160 degrees Fahrenheit during the peak phase. Rodale Institute notes that these temperatures are critical for killing weed seeds and pathogens — which is what separates hot compost from passive, cold-pile decomposition. Cold composting works, but slowly and without pathogen reduction.

Moisture is the other variable to check. Pull a handful of material from the interior. Squeeze it. You should get a few drops of water — if that. More than that and the pile is too wet. No moisture at all and it's too dry. The standard is "like a wrung-out sponge." Too wet and you lose oxygen, which kicks off anaerobic conditions and bad smells. Too dry and the bacteria simply stop working — your pile goes quiet and cold.

If the pile is too wet, add dry carbon material and turn it to aerate. If it's too dry, water it lightly and mix the water through the pile so it reaches the interior.

sections.heading: Turning: The Secret to Finishing Fast
sections.body:
The single most powerful lever you have over a compost pile's speed is turning.

Turning does three things. It reintroduces oxygen to the pile, which keeps the aerobic microbial community active. It redistributes heat so that outer material, which cools faster, gets moved to the center where temperatures are higher. And it mixes materials together so the whole pile breaks down evenly rather than leaving pockets of unprocessed material.

An actively hot pile benefits from turning every three to five days during the peak phase. That sounds like a lot, but each turning takes maybe fifteen minutes with a good pitchfork. And a pile that's turned regularly can go from raw materials to finished compost in four to six weeks — versus four to twelve months for a pile that's never turned.

Here's what I watch for when I turn: if the center is still screaming hot and the outer material is still recognizable, turn it to rotate fresh material into the heat. If the whole pile is starting to even out temperature-wise and the material is dark and crumbly throughout, you're entering the curing phase. At that point, you can turn less frequently — once a week is fine — and let the pile mature.

Curing matters. Even after the hot phase, finished compost needs a few weeks of rest to stabilize. Immature compost can actually harm plants because it continues breaking down in soil, tying up nitrogen in the process. Mature, cured compost has completed that process and is safe to apply anywhere.

sections.heading: What Done Looks Like
sections.body:
Finished compost is dark — almost black. It's crumbly, not slimy. It smells like rich earth. You can't identify any of the original materials in it. The pile has shrunk to roughly a third of its original volume.

Those are the visual and olfactory tests. The squeeze test is also useful: take a handful and squeeze it. It should hold together briefly, then crumble when you release it. If it's slimy or wet, it needs more time and aeration. If it's dusty and breaks apart without holding shape, it may be getting too dry during curing.

Cured compost is one of the most valuable things you can add to a garden. It's not a fertilizer in the conventional NPK sense. It's a soil amendment — it improves structure, drainage, water retention, and most importantly, biological activity. Gabe Brown, one of the pioneers of regenerative agriculture, talks about the importance of feeding the soil food web rather than feeding plants directly. Finished compost feeds that web. It's teeming with life.

I spread finished compost on my beds as a top dressing, work it into planting holes, and use it as the base for my container mixes. I never have enough of it. That's how you know it's good.

Start your pile today. Gather your browns, gather your greens, layer them up, and check it in two days. If it's hot in the center, you're already on your way to some of the best soil you've ever grown anything in.
